title: The doorway to
a

heart.
lead: Notes from the surgeon's pen — heart health, surgical journeys and patient stories.

midcab-explained.title: What is MIDCAB? Bypass without opening the sternum
midcab-explained.excerpt: Coronary bypass through a small incision under the left breast — how it works, who's a candidate, and how recovery differs.

body.p: When most people picture coronary bypass, they imagine a long incision down the middle of the chest. MIDCAB — Minimally Invasive Direct Coronary Artery Bypass — skips that step. The surgeon works through a 6–8 cm cut under the left breast; the sternum stays whole.
MIDCAB is ideal for patients with single-vessel disease in the LAD. With multi-vessel involvement, a hybrid approach — MIDCAB + percutaneous stent — may be considered.
Because the sternum isn't broken, post-op pain is significantly lower. Most patients are discharged in 3–4 days, return to office work in 2 weeks, and to heavy activity in 4–6 weeks.

body.quote: Reaching the target vessel is the same goal in every bypass technique; what differs is how we get there.

body.p: Not every patient is a candidate. Anatomical position, lung adhesions, prior surgical history all matter at the planning stage.

tavi-vs-savr.title: TAVI or open aortic valve replacement?
tavi-vs-savr.excerpt: Choosing between transcatheter and surgical valve replacement in aortic stenosis — risk, age, anatomy, durability.

body.p: TAVI has been the most important advance in aortic valve treatment of the last decade. A new valve is delivered through a catheter — usually from the leg — without opening the chest.
Originally for patients at very high surgical risk. Today it's also used safely in intermediate- and low-risk older patients.

body.h2: When is open surgery still preferred?

body.p: Younger patients, those needing concomitant procedures, complex anatomy, or bicuspid valves — SAVR remains the standard.

body.quote: TAVI and SAVR aren't rivals; they're two tools for the same goal. Matching patient to tool is the surgeon's job.

what-is-bentall.title: Bentall procedure: Replacing the aortic root as one
what-is-bentall.excerpt: When aortic root aneurysm and severe valve insufficiency coexist, Bentall solves both in a single operation.

body.p: Bentall replaces the aortic valve, root and ascending aorta in one composite graft. Typical candidate: a patient with a dilated root and severe valve insufficiency.
Diseased tissue removed; a tubular graft containing a mechanical or biological valve is inserted; coronary ostia are reimplanted onto the graft.
Modern series report 30-day mortality below 2%. Long-term survival depends directly on valve choice and patient compliance.

off-pump-bypass.title: Off-pump bypass: Surgery on the beating heart
off-pump-bypass.excerpt: Bypass without the heart-lung machine — advantages, limits, and patient selection.

body.p: Off-pump CABG keeps the heart beating during the operation; specialised stabilisers immobilise the target vessel area only.

patient-journey-de.title: From Munich to Antalya: A patient's journey
patient-journey-de.excerpt: Following our 64-year-old patient through the full TAVI pathway — from first video call to return home.

body.p: K., 64, came to us from Munich with severe aortic stenosis. The journey began with a video consultation built around the echo and CT his local cardiologist had sent us.
Imaging confirmed TAVI eligibility; surgery was scheduled two weeks later. The clinic coordinated everything — hospital, transfer, accommodation.
The procedure took 90 minutes. He stood up the same day and was discharged on day three. A German interpreter joined every consult and patient-education session.

after-surgery-life.title: Life after open-heart surgery: The first 12 weeks
after-surgery-life.excerpt: A practical week-by-week calendar for the months after discharge — when to walk, when to drive, when to lift.

body.p: The hours on the operating table shape the rest of your life — but the result depends just as much on the first twelve weeks after.

aortic-dissection-101.title: Aortic dissection: Anatomy of an emergency
aortic-dissection-101.excerpt: Where minutes matter, not hours. Symptoms, types, and modern surgical approaches.

body.p: Aortic dissection begins with a tear in the inner layer of the aortic wall.

atrial-fib-maze.title: MAZE: A surgical answer to atrial fibrillation
atrial-fib-maze.excerpt: How carefully drawn lines on the heart's chambers can restore rhythm when drugs and ablation fall short.

body.p: MAZE creates controlled conduction lines on cardiac tissue to interrupt fibrillation.

sutureless-valves.title: Sutureless aortic valves: Shorter time, lower risk
sutureless-valves.excerpt: Valves that hold without traditional stitches reduce operating time and risk in older patients.

body.p: Sutureless valves are bioprostheses that anchor with radial force rather than sutures.
